request.question: Girl Alone was sponsored by an American multinational food manufacturing company that produces cereal and convenience foods, and is headquartered where?

title: La Loma Foods
Tee Yih Jia
Ken's Foods
Kellogg's
GoodMark Foods
PepsiCo
Convenience food
Bright Food
Patties Foods
Girl Alone
sentences: La Loma Foods, formerly named Loma Linda Food Company and Loma Linda Foods, and with products presently branded under the name Loma Linda and Loma, is a former food manufacturing company that produced vegetarian and vegan foods.
It is presently an active brand of vegetarian and vegan food products produced and purveyed by the Atlantic Natural Foods Company of Nashville, North Carolina.
Loma Linda Foods began operations in 1905 under the name The Sanitarium Food Company and was owned by the Seventh-day Adventist Church until 1990.
Tee Yih Jia Food Manufacturing Pte Ltd (simplified Chinese: 第一家), is a food manufacturing company headquartered in Singapore.

Tee Yih Jia is the world leading manufacturer of spring roll pastry (also known as "popiah" in local context) that also manufactures a wide range of ready-to-eat Asian convenience foods such as roti paratha, crepes, cocktail prawn rolls, glutinous rice balls, spring rolls and samosas.

Apart from pastry-based food, Tee Yih Jia manufactures other frozen convenience food products such as Dim Sum, Ramen, Oriental Buns (or mantou), and Asian Pizzas with flavours like Peking duck and satay chicken.
Tee Yih Jia's "Spring Home" brand products are manufactured in Singapore as well as in several regional facilities.
Tee Yih Jia has a 400000 sqft factory in Senoko (Singapore) and a Johor (Malaysia) factory of 200000 sqft , as well as manufacturing plants in the USA and China.
Ken's Foods is a privately held food manufacturing company located in Marlborough, Massachusetts.
Ken's primary products are salad dressings, sauces, and marinades.
The company's commercial food manufacturing divisions produce products both for retail sale and food service, including contract manufacturing for companies such as Newman's Own.
The Kellogg Company (also Kellogg's, Kellogg, and Kellogg's of Battle Creek) is an American multinational food manufacturing company headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States.
Kellogg's produces cereal and convenience foods, including cookies, crackers, toaster pastries, cereal bars, fruit-flavored snacks, frozen waffles, and vegetarian foods.

The company's brands include Froot Loops, Apple Jacks, Corn Flakes, Frosted Flakes, Rice Krispies, Special K, Cocoa Krispies, Keebler, Pringles, Pop-Tarts, Kashi, Cheez-It, Eggo, Nutri-Grain, Morningstar Farms, and many more.
Kellogg's stated purpose is "Nourishing families so they can flourish and thrive."
Goodmark Foods, Inc. is an American food manufacturing company, based in Raleigh, North Carolina.
It produces "meat snacks, packaged meats, and extruded grain snacks," especially Slim Jim jerky meat snacks.
It is owned by ConAgra.
PepsiCo, Inc. is an American multinational food, snack, and beverage corporation headquartered in Purchase, New York.
PepsiCo has interests in the manufacturing, marketing, and distribution of grain-based snack foods, beverages, and other products.
PepsiCo was formed in 1965 with the merger of the Pepsi-Cola Company and Frito-Lay, Inc.

PepsiCo has since expanded from its namesake product Pepsi to a broader range of food and beverage brands, the largest of which included an acquisition of Tropicana Products in 1998 and the Quaker Oats Company in 2001, which added the Gatorade brand to its portfolio.
Convenience food, or tertiary processed food, is food that is commercially prepared (often through processing) to optimise ease of consumption.
Such food is usually ready to eat without further preparation.
It may also be easily portable, have a long shelf life, or offer a combination of such convenient traits.
Although restaurant meals meet this definition, the term is seldom applied to them.
Convenience foods include ready-to-eat dry products, frozen foods such as TV dinners, shelf-stable foods, prepared mixes such as cake mix, and snack foods.
Bright Food (officially Bright Food (Group) Co., Ltd.) is a multinational food and beverages manufacturing company headquartered in Shanghai, China.
It is the second-largest China-based food manufacturing company measured by 2011 revenues.
The company is wholly owned by the Shanghai Municipal Government via Shanghai Municipal Investment Group and another sister company.
Patties Foods, is an Australian food manufacturing company that produces meat pies, baked goods, frozen fruits, and pre-made desserts.
Patties Foods is represented in the Australian market by the Four'N Twenty, Patties, Herbert Adams, Creative Gourmet, Nanna's, Chefs Pride and Snowy River brands.
Patties is the largest Meat pie producing company in Australia, and the world.
They are the producers of several well-known Meat pie's, including the Patties, Snowy River pie, Herbert Adams pie, and as of 2003, Four'N Twenty pies.
Patties Foods is headquartered in Bairnsdale, Victoria.
Girl Alone was an American radio soap opera broadcast on NBC from 1935 to 1941.
Sponsored by Kellogg's and Quaker Oats, the series was scripted by Fayette Krum.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Girl Alone was sponsored by an American multinational food manufacturing company that produces cereal and convenience foods, and is headquartered where?
hop1_docs: Kellogg's | The Kellogg Company (also Kellogg's, Kellogg, and Kellogg's of Battle Creek) is an American multinational food manufacturing company headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States.  Kellogg's produces cereal and convenience foods, including cookies, crackers, toaster pastries, cereal bars, fruit-flavored snacks, frozen waffles, and vegetarian foods.  The company's brands include Froot Loops, Apple Jacks, Corn Flakes, Frosted Flakes, Rice Krispies, Special K, Cocoa Krispies, Keebler, Pringles, Pop-Tarts, Kashi, Cheez-It, Eggo, Nutri-Grain, Morningstar Farms, and many more.  Kellogg's stated purpose is "Nourishing families so they can flourish and thrive."

Bright Food | Bright Food (officially Bright Food (Group) Co., Ltd.) is a multinational food and beverages manufacturing company headquartered in Shanghai, China.  It is the second-largest China-based food manufacturing company measured by 2011 revenues.  The company is wholly owned by the Shanghai Municipal Government via Shanghai Municipal Investment Group and another sister company.

Flavour and Life | Flavour and Life (FAL) Food and Beverages is a multinational food and beverage company headquartered in Sydney, Australia, with interests in the manufacturing, producing, marketing, and distribution of health-based foods, beverages, cosmetics, and other products.  It is best known for its Coco Joy line of coconut-based foods and beverages.
Siniora (company) | Siniora Food and Manufacturing plc.  Is a multinational food industry company that produces meat products in Palestine, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia.

Convenient Food Mart | Convenient Food Mart (CFM) is a chain of convenience stores in the United States.  The private company's headquarters are located in Mentor, Ohio, and there are currently approximately 325 stores located in the US.  Convenient Food Mart operates on the franchise system.

Bell Food Group | The Bell Food Group is the leading meat processor and manufacturer of convenience products in Switzerland.  The company was founded in 1869 by Samuel Bell in Basel, where its headquarters is located up to now.  Its range of products includes meat, poultry, charcuterie, seafood and convenience products.  Brands include Bell, Abraham, ZIMBO, Môssieur Polette and Hilcona.  Some 8,000 employees generated consolidated sales of CHF 2.6 billion in the fiscal year 2014.  Bell is listed on the Swiss stock exchange.  The majority shareholder of Bell is Coop, one of Switzerland's largest retail and wholesale companies.

McKee Foods | McKee Foods Corporation is a privately held and family owned American snack food and granola manufacturer headquartered in Collegedale, Tennessee.  The corporation is the maker of the Little Debbie Snacks, Sunbelt Bakery granola and cereal, Heartland Brands, and Drake's Cakes.  McKee Foods has its own fleet of trucks to distribute its products.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found: Passage [1] contains a direct match to the company description: "Kellogg's | The Kellogg Company" and states it "produces cereal and convenience foods" and is "headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States." (quotes from [1]: "produces cereal and convenience foods"; "headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States.")
- Ambiguities: The provided passages do not mention "Girl Alone" at all (no candidate page found). The Kellogg company appears with variants in [1]: "The Kellogg Company" (also "Kellogg's", "Kellogg", "Kellogg's of Battle Creek").
- Bridge entity / Missing fact: Bridge entity = "Kellogg's | The Kellogg Company" (as in passage [1]). Missing fact needed = verification that "Girl Alone" was sponsored by Kellogg's (i.e., an explicit sponsorship statement linking Girl Alone to Kellogg's); once verified, the precise headquarters detail for that sponsor should be extracted/confirmed.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Girl Alone" page (or "Girl Alone (radio program)" / any disambiguation) and check the lede/infobox and sections titled "Sponsorship", "Broadcast history", or "Production" for the named sponsor. Search keywords: "Girl Alone sponsor", "Girl Alone sponsored by Kellogg".
  2) Open "Kellogg's" / "The Kellogg Company" page and check the lede/infobox and "Overview"/"Headquarters" for the canonical headquarters statement (verify "Battle Creek, Michigan, United States" as in [1]).
  3) If "Girl Alone" lists a different sponsor, fetch that sponsor's page and check its lede/infobox for headquarters.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and the sponsorship is confirmed.
response.hop2_query: Girl Alone radio program sponsor Kellogg
hop2_docs: Girl Alone | Girl Alone was an American radio soap opera broadcast on NBC from 1935 to 1941.  Sponsored by Kellogg's and Quaker Oats, the series was scripted by Fayette Krum.

Pep (cereal) | Pep was a brand of whole-wheat breakfast cereal produced by the Kellogg Company, and introduced in 1923.  Pep was a long-running rival to Wheaties, and also the sponsor of Mutual Radio's "The Adventures of Superman" radio series.  One of Pep's advertising slogans was "the Sunshine cereal".

Kellogg-HKUST Executive MBA Program | Established in 1998, the Kellogg-HKUST Executive MBA Program of the School of Business and Management (also known as HKUST Business School) is a partnership program between the Kellogg School of Management at Northwestern University (Kellogg) and the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology's School (HKUST).

Kellogg School of Management | The Kellogg School of Management (The Kellogg School or Kellogg) is the business school of Northwestern University in Evanston, Illinois, with additional campuses in downtown Chicago, Illinois and Miami, Florida.  Kellogg offers full-time, part-time, and executive programs, and partners with schools in China, France/Singapore, India, Spain, Hong Kong, Israel, Germany, Canada, and Thailand.  Degrees granted include the Master of Business Administration (MBA), Ph.D. programs in several fields, an MBA-JD, and MMM Program, a MBA + MDI dual degree.  The MDI degree replaces the MEM degree within the MMM program.

Rosemary (radio) | Rosemary is an American radio soap opera broadcast on NBC Radio from October 2, 1944 to March 23, 1945, and on CBS Radio from March 26, 1945 to July 1, 1955.  Starring Betty Winkler as Rosemary Dawson Roberts, the program's only sponsor was Procter & Gamble, primarily for Ivory Snow dishwashing liquid, Camay soap, Dash and Tide laundry detergents and Prell shampoo.  The series was created by Elaine Carrington, who had previously created "Pepper Young's Family" (1932-1959) and "When a Girl Marries" (1939-1957).

Escape (radio program) | Escape was radio's leading anthology series of high-adventure radio dramas, airing on CBS from July 7, 1947 to September 25, 1954.  Since the program did not have a regular sponsor like "Suspense", it was subjected to frequent schedule shifts and lower production budgets, although Richfield Oil signed on as a sponsor for five months in 1950.

Just Plain Bill | Just Plain Bill was a long-running 15-minute American daytime radio drama program heard on CBS Radio and NBC Radio.  The series was sponsored by Anacin for 18 of the program's 23-year run.  Other sponsors over the years were Kolynos toothpaste, Clapp’s baby food, and BiSoDol.  It was “the real-life story of people just like people we all know.”
response.summary_2: The Kellogg Company produces cereal and convenience foods and is headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States. Girl Alone was sponsored by Kellogg's and Quaker Oats.
